---
title: "R Notebook"
output:
  html_document:
    df_print: paged
editor_options:
  markdown:
    wrap: 72
---

This is an [R Markdown](http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com) Notebook. When you
execute code within the notebook, the results appear beneath the code.

```{r include=TRUE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(results = "hold")
```

# Data Preparation

```{r Libraries}
# install.packages("factoextra")
# install.packages("dplyr")
# install.packages("ggrepel")
# install.packages("tidyverse")
# install.packages("dendextend")
# install.packages("MASS")
# install.packages("ggtext")

library(factoextra)
library(dplyr)
library(ggrepel)
library(tidyverse)
library(tidytable)
library(cluster)
library(combinat)
library(dendextend)
library(MASS)
library(multcomp)
library(ggtext)
```

(For the above ggrepel library, see
<https://github.com/slowkow/ggrepel>)

```{r Get postagged_only.csv. Big data!}
setwd("~/GitHub/cltk-2025-atticus")

dims <- c(
  "title", "author", "path", "form", "lemma", "tag", "Aspect", "Mood", "Number",
  "Person",
  "Tense",
  "VerbForm",
  "Voice",
  "Case",
  "PronType",
  "Gender",
  "Polarity",
  "Degree",
  "NumType"
)

data <- read.csv("postagged-texts.csv", na.strings = "", col.names = dims, stringsAsFactors = TRUE)

summary(data)
```

One thing I want to note is that not every value is relevant. I'll do
this further below and not here, but I want to go one at a time and see
whether Degree [c("Abs", "Cmp")], Gender [c("Fem", "Masc", "Neut")], or
Case (c("Nom", "Acc", "Abl", "Gen", "Dat", "Loc", "Voc", "Abs")) make
any difference. Some potential issues are also that there are
redundancies in the variables. If we want to do significance testing, we
want to know each variable is independent. So, if we get rid of POS
tags, we should still keep SCONJ, ADP, CCONJ,

```{r}
# data <- data[rowSums(data == 'M. Tullius Cicero', na.rm = TRUE) == 0 | data$title %in% c("Letters to Atticus"),]
data <- data[rowSums(data == "M. Tullius Cicero", na.rm = TRUE) == 0 | data$title %in% c("Letters to Atticus", "Epistulae ad Familiares", "Letters to and from Brutus", "Letters to and from Quintus"), ] # This version includes Cicero's other letters

# Rename "Imp" to "Imperative" in mood category
levels(data$Mood) <- c(levels(data$Mood), "Imperative")
sequence <- which(data$Mood == "Imp")
data$Mood[sequence] <- "Imperative"
remove(sequence)

data$Mood[data$Mood == "Imp"] <- "Imperative"
data$Mood <- droplevels(data$Mood) # Remove the old "Imp

titles <- unique(data$title)
num_words <- table(data$title)
final.frame <- data.frame(title = titles)
```

Now that we have the data, what we're *really* interested in is how many
of each tag appears in each work (how many of each POS, how many perfect
verbs, whatever). The point is, we're not interested in every individual
word.

This block of code will loop through and create a new data frame called
***final.frame*** (which is probably poorly named, but I'll fix it later
or I won't). This data frame has a variable for the count of each tag,
and every row is a unique work.

```{r Create final.frame}
for (i in 1:length(titles)) {
  frame <- data[data$title == titles[i], ]
  frame <- subset(frame, select = c(-form, -path, -NumType, -author, -lemma))
  for (n in 2:length(frame)) {
    t <- table(frame[, n])
    # t <- prop.table(t)
    names <- names(t)

    for (name_index in seq(1, length(names))) {
      final.frame[i, names[name_index]] <- unname(t[names[name_index]])
    }
  }
}

# Normalize by number of words in each work
for (i in 2:length(final.frame[1, ])) {
  for (n in 1:length(final.frame[, 1])) {
    final.frame[n, i] <- final.frame[n, i] * (10000 / num_words[[final.frame[n, ]$title]])
  }
}
```

A few things will make our life easier. We don't want any NAs in there,
because that'll gum up the clustering. We'll replace any missing values
with 0, because 0 occurrences of a variable is the only reason there
would be NA values at this stage.

I have a *hopefully* temporary measure where I replace row 46's title
with Apocolocynthosis. The title is given as "Petronius. Seneca
Apocolocynthosis" because it's based off a Loeb edition of that name.
However, when I abbreviate the names at the end of this block, this
makes it ambiguous with the other Perseus text which contains just
Petronius, and **rownames()** doesn't allow duplicates.

I also remove Cicero's works other than the Letters to Atticus. The
reason is that the clustering algorithm greatly prefers to put works by
the same author together. This is great, but we're not interested in the
authorship of the Letters. We're interested in how similar it is to
other texts of the same genre. So, Cicero has to go.

The numbers aren't normalized according to the size of their respective
works yet, so I've divided each number by the total number of words in
the given work in the next chunk. I multiplied each by 100,000 too, just
to get numbers that look a little nicer and aren't in scientific
notation.

Finally, we do what I said above, turning the rownames into the title. I
shorten them to 12 characters max.

```{r Clean up final.frame}
# Removed punctuation because PCA showed that it was an "important" variable
final.frame <- subset(final.frame, select = c(-PUNCT, -X, -VERB, -Sing, -Plur))

# Move Pliny's letters close to the top
row.one <- final.frame[final.frame$title %in% c("Letters"), ]
other.rows <- final.frame[final.frame$title %in% c("Letters") == FALSE, ]
final.frame <- rbind(row.one, other.rows)
rownames(final.frame) <- 1:length(final.frame$title)

# Move letters to Atticus to the top
row.one <- final.frame[final.frame$title %in% c("Letters to Atticus"), ]
other.rows <- final.frame[final.frame$title %in% c("Letters to Atticus") == FALSE, ]
final.frame <- rbind(row.one, other.rows)
rownames(final.frame) <- 1:length(final.frame$title) # Replace the rownames with the new correct number
remove(other.rows)
remove(row.one)


# Clean up NAs and filter out Cicero's works that are not the Letters to Atticus
final.frame[is.na(final.frame)] <- 0

# Add a number to make

# CAN GET RID OF THIS LATER WHEN I FIX TITLES
# final.frame$title[46] <- "Apocolocyntosis"
# final.frame <- final.frame[grepl("Horace", final.frame$title) == FALSE,]

# cicero_works_nonatt <- c("M. Tulli Ciceronis Orationes", "Librorum de Re Publica Sex", "M. Tulli Ciceronis\nscripta quae manserunt omnia,\nfasc. 43", "de Natura Deorum", "M. Tullii Ciceronis opera quae supersunt omnia", "Tusculanae Disputationes", "M. Tulli Ciceronis Rhetorica, Tomus II", "De Senectute De Amicitia De Divinatione, With An English Translation", "Epistulae ad Familiares", "Letters to and from Quintus")

# #final.frame <- final.frame[(final.frame$title %in% cicero_works_nonatt) == FALSE,]
#
#
# #These values give NA, cause issues
# final.frame[c("Neg", "Rcp", "Art")] <- NULL

for (i in 1:length(final.frame$title)) {
  final.frame$title <- as.character(final.frame$title)
  final.frame$title[i] <- paste(as.character(i), final.frame$title[i])
}

rownames(final.frame) <- final.frame$title
final.frame$title <- lapply(X = final.frame$title, FUN = substr, start = 1, stop = 16)
rownames(final.frame) <- lapply(X = rownames(final.frame), FUN = substr, start = 1, stop = 16)
```

Now that we have a viable DF, let's cut it up a little bit. We don't
want the first column, because that's all text. Not every clustering
algorithm needs the DF to be pre-scaled (that is, subtracting the mean
from every value and dividing it by the standard deviation to make the
comparable). However, some do expect the data to be scaled, so lets
create two variables. **df_clust** isn't scaled, and **df_scaled** is.

This is also where I

```{r df_clust & df_scaled}
df_clust <- final.frame[, 2:length(final.frame)]
# df_clust <- slice_sample(df_clust, n=45)

df_scaled <- as.data.frame(scale(df_clust))
```

# Analysis

First, to get some of the formatting predefined.

Another note is that here is where I remove the variables I'm not
considering.

```{r}
# 10 variables to use for analysis. This is based of the work done in the Testing section below
# vars <- c("2", "PART", "Imp", "Pres", "Part", "Abl", "Pass", "Fut", "1", "Prs")

# These vars I selected based on the Better variable selection heading down below.
# These are the variables that belonged to the 30 with the highest frequency AND the highest variance
# I also removed Act because it isn't independent from passive verbs, and I removed gender because there's no reason to believe it's related to register.
vars <- c("Pres", "NOUN", "Imp", "2", "Past", "Perf", "3", "PRON", "Fin", "Nom", "Abl", "Prs", "Acc", "ADJ", "Gen", "ADP", "Part", "Pass", "DET", "Rel")

df_clust <- df_clust %>% dplyr::select(all_of(vars))
rownames(df_clust) <- rownames(final.frame)
df_scaled <- df_scaled %>% dplyr::select(all_of(vars))
rownames(df_scaled) <- rownames(df_scaled)

# Add this with the + operator to fviz_cluster plots. @param data is the original dataset used for cluster analysis (df_clust in the first case)
cluster_labels <- geom_text_repel(aes(label = rownames(data)), vjust = "inward", hjust = "inward", max.overlaps = 20, size = 3)

# Args for fviz_clust
palette <- c("#2E9FDF", "#00AFBB", "#E7B800", "red", "gray")
geom <- "point"
ellipse.type <- "convex"
ggtheme <- theme_bw()

# K-means
k <- 4

# Hcut
hc_func <- "agnes"
hc_method <- "ward"
hc_metric <- "euclidean"
cex <- 0.2

# Text labels for distance diagrams. @param label is usually the rownames of a data.frame
margin_size <- 10
distance_labels <- theme(axis.text = element_text(size = 5, vjust = 1), margins = margin(t = margin_size, r = margin_size, b = margin_size, l = margin_size))

# Dendrogram theme
dend_size <- 0.2
dend_theme <- theme(axis.text = element_text(size = dend_size, vjust = 1), margins = margin(t = margin_size, r = margin_size, b = margin_size, l = margin_size))
```

## K-means clustering

Time to cluster! K-means is a good starting point here. I'll check out
CLARA as well. We'll need to evaluate what's best. Here's the
modifications we can make:

1.  Change which variables we account for.

2.  Change the number of centers (between 3 and 5? But we already have a
    way of visualizing that)

3.  Change the measuring method (i.e. Euclidean

4.  Change the algorithm

```{r K-means clustering}
set.seed(126126)
kmeans_basic_cluster <- kmeans(df_clust, centers = k, nstart = 25)
kmeans_basic_table <- data.frame(kmeans_basic_cluster$size, kmeans_basic_cluster$centers)
kmeans_basic_df <- data.frame(Clusters = kmeans_basic_cluster$cluster, df_clust)
```

Let's do our first visualization. Four clusters seems like a reasonable
amount, looking at the fviz_nbclust results with the wss method.

```{r}
# TODO ADD SUMMARIES OF THE ABOVE DATA HERE

# Principal component analysis

pca <- prcomp(df_clust)

summary(pca)

# Visualizations

fviz_nbclust(df_clust, kmeans, method = "wss", nstart = 50)

dim1 <- as.character(summary(pca)$importance[2] * 100)
dim2 <- as.character(summary(pca)$importance[5] * 100)

cluster_main <- fviz_cluster(kmeans_basic_cluster,
  data = df_clust,
  palette = palette,
  geom = geom,
  ellipse.type = ellipse.type,
  ggtheme = ggtheme
) +
  geom_text_repel(
    aes(label = rownames(df_clust)),
    vjust = "inward",
    hjust = "inward",
    max.overlaps = 14,
    size = 2,
    seed = 12927
  ) +
  labs(
    x = paste(strwrap(paste("Dim1", dim1, "(Left = more 1st/2nd person, imperfective verbs, Right = more Ablative nouns, perfective verbs, and nouns overall)"), 70),
      collapse = "\n"
    ),
    y = paste(strwrap(paste("Dim2", dim2, "(Up = more 3rd person and past tense verbs, Down = more nouns)"), 50), collapse = "\n"),
    title = "(1) K-means clustering applied to PHI Latin Texts, k=4"
  ) +
  theme(axis.title = element_text(size = 12)) +
  annotate("text", x = -7, y = -0.5, label = "Comedy", fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = -2.5, y = 4, label = "Prose", fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = -4, y = -5, label = "Poetry", fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = 4, y = -4, label = "History", fontface = "bold")

cluster_main


ggsave(filename = "Images/cluster-diagram-kmeans.png", dpi = "print")

cluster_main <- fviz_cluster(kmeans_basic_cluster,
  data = df_clust,
  palette = palette,
  geom = geom,
  ellipse.type = ellipse.type,
  ggtheme = ggtheme
) +
  geom_text_repel(aes(label = rownames(df_clust)), vjust = "inward", hjust = "inward", max.overlaps = 12, size = 2, seed = 12927) +
  labs(
    x = paste(strwrap(paste("Dim1", dim1, "(Left = more 1st/2nd person, imperfective verbs, Right = more ablative nouns, perfective verbs, and nouns overall)"), 70), collapse = "\n"),
    y = paste(strwrap(paste("Dim2", dim2, "(Up = more 3rd person and past tense verbs, Down = more nouns)"), 35), collapse = "\n"),
    title = "(1) K-means clustering applied to PHI Latin Texts, k=4"
  ) +
  theme(axis.title = element_text(size = 15)) + annotate("text", x = -7, y = -0.5, label = "Comedy", fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = -2.5, y = 4, label = "Prose", fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = -4, y = -5, label = "Poetry", fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = 4, y = -4, label = "History", fontface = "bold")
cluster_main

ggsave(filename = "Images/cluster-diagram-kmeans-LARGEPRINT.png", dpi = "print")

str(kmeans_basic_cluster)


distance <- get_dist(df_scaled)
fviz_dist(distance, gradient = list(low = "#00AFBB", mid = "white", high = "#FC4E07")) + distance_labels
ggsave(filename = "Images/Distance plot with main d.f.png", dpi = "print")
```

The graph above should be the first visualization in the presentation.
Next, we want to see what sets the Letters and other similar texts apart
from the others.

The following graph represents the loadings for the X axis of the
cluster analysis above. The farther we go to one side, the more 1st/2nd
person verbs, personal pronouns, present/future tense verbs, and
imperfective aspect verbs there are. Let's talk about what that means.

Person corresponds to a change in tenor accompanied by colloquial texts.
Tenor, as a term, refers to the author and their relationship to their
audience. Comedy, letters, and works of Seneca which come very close to
that cluster have an intimate audience (in the case of Comedy and
Seneca, this is an affectation. In such a close situation, the goal is
to both get information from the addressee but also communicate
information about themselves.

This information about person is fairly obvious, but the situation with
personal pronouns is less obvious. Latin has no need of personal
pronouns; the grammars say they tend to be used for emphasis or
...(*cite Allen & Greenough and Kühner / Stegmann*).

The information about tense is also expected. Speakers in colloquial
texts have a variety of purposes: asking and answering questions,
demonstrating their current thoughts and feelings, making plans, and so
on. These cover a broad range of statements, each of which requires a
different type of verb (*examples here*).

To sum up, texts on the left-hand side of the graph have a highly
personal tenor with ....FILL IN HERE, RE-READ BIBER

Texts on the the right-hand side of the cluster graph feature more
ablative nouns, participles, and passive verbs.

```{r}
pca <- prcomp(df_clust)

rotations.df <- as.data.frame(pca$rotation)

rotations <- data.frame(var = rownames(rotations.df), rotations = rotations.df$PC1)

rotation.two <- data.frame(var = rownames(rotations.df), rotations = rotations.df$PC2)

ggplot(rotations, aes(var, rotations)) +
  labs(x = "Variables", y = "Correlation Strength", title = "(2) Variation across X-axis in cluster diagram") +
  geom_col() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 15, angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 1),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14),
    title = element_text(size = 14)
  )
ggsave(filename = "Images/PC1_rotations.png", dpi = "print")

ggplot(rotations, aes(var, rotations)) +
  labs(x = "Variables", y = "Correlation Strength", title = "(2) Variation across X-axis in cluster diagram") +
  geom_col() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 15, angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 1),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14),
    title = element_text(size = 14)
  )
ggsave(filename = "Images/PC1_rotations.png", dpi = "print")

ggplot(rotation.two, aes(var, rotations)) +
  labs(x = "Variables", y = "Correlation Strength", title = "Correlation of Y-axis in cluster diagram with selected variables") +
  geom_col() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 15, angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 1)
  ) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 15, angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 1)
  )
ggsave(filename = "Images/PC2_rotations.png", dpi = "print")

# NOTE: I should try this with Act as a variable too. Pass alone might be high because there are a lot of participles and ablative nouns.
```

How different are the values of Atticus compared to the means of its own
cluster?

```{r}
# Lets get a vector of each of the Att values minus the mean of its cluster.

cluster_four <- kmeans_basic_df |> filter(Clusters == 1)

dist <- cluster_four |>
  dplyr::select(-title) |>
  map(
    \(x) x[1] - mean(x[-1])
  )

dist_df <- data.frame(var = names(dist)[-1], distance = unlist(dist)[-1])

ggplot(dist_df, aes(var, distance)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_continuous(breaks = c(0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250)) +
  labs(title = "(3) Difference Between Letters to Atticus and Its Cluster") +
  xlab("Variable (X1 and X2 = 1st person and 2nd person)") +
  ylab("Letters to Atticus minus cluster mean") +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 15, angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 1),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14),
    title = element_text(size = 14)
  )

ggsave(filename = "Images/att-vs-same-cluster-means.png", dpi = "print")
```

What about the differences between Comedy and the Letters to Atticus?

```{r}
kmeans_basic_df$title <- as.vector(final.frame$title)
cluster_four <- kmeans_basic_df |>
  filter(Clusters == 4 | title == "1 Letters to Att") |>
  dplyr::select(-title)

dist <- cluster_four |>
  map(
    \(x) x[1] - mean(x[-1])
  )

dist_df <- data.frame(var = names(dist)[-1], distance = unlist(dist)[-1])

ggplot(dist_df, aes(var, distance)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_continuous(breaks = c(0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250)) +
  labs(title = "(4) Difference Between Letters to Atticus and Comedy") +
  xlab("Variable (X1 and X2 = 1st person and 2nd person)") +
  ylab("Letters to Atticus minus cluster mean") +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 15, angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 1),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14),
    title = element_text(size = 14)
  )

ggsave(filename = "Images/atticus-vs-comedy-means.png", dpi = "print")
```

I commented this out because I'm done with it, but I saved the results
in a data table. Because K-means has an element of randomness, the
results aren't identical every time, but Hartigan-Wong, the default
algorithm, either had the lowest or tied for the lowest total wihin sum
of squares.

+---------------------+-------------------+
| a l g orithm        | t o t . w i       |
|                     | thinss            |
| \                   |                   |
| \<chr\>             | \                 |
|                     | \<dbl\>           |
+:====================+==================:+
| H a r t i g a       | 3 9 88.635        |
| n-Wong              |                   |
+---------------------+-------------------+
| Lloyd               | 3 9 89.989        |
+---------------------+-------------------+
| Forgy               | 3 9 93.158        |
+---------------------+-------------------+
| M a cQueen          | 3 9 89.989        |
+---------------------+-------------------+

```{r}
#
# algorithms <- c("Hartigan-Wong", "Lloyd", "Forgy", "MacQueen")
#
# algorithms.tot.withinss <- vector(mode = "double")
# for (i in 1:length(algorithms)) {
#   algorithms.tot.withinss[i] <- kmeans(df_scaled, centers=k, nstart=25, algorithm = algorithms[i])$tot.withinss
# }
#
# data.frame(algorithm = algorithms, tot.withinss = algorithms.tot.withinss)
```

But, let's look at the cluster that contains the letters to Atticus.
Maybe a bar chart showing facets of each?

```{r}
att_clust <- kmeans_basic_df$Clusters[rownames(kmeans_basic_df) == "22 Letters t"]

# df.barplot <- data.frame(names = dimnames(kmeans_basic_df)[[2]][-1], attclust = sapply(X=kmeans_basic_df[kmeans_basic_df$Clusters == att_clust,], sum)[-1])

# df.barplot$poetclust <-  sapply(X=kmeans_basic_df[kmeans_basic_df$Clusters == 2,], sum)[-1]

df.barplot <- data.frame(cluster = 1, var = dimnames(kmeans_basic_df)[[2]][-1], clustersum = scale(sapply(X = kmeans_basic_df[kmeans_basic_df$Clusters == 1, ], mean)[-1]))
for (i in 2:max(kmeans_basic_df$Clusters)) {
  temp <- data.frame(cluster = i, var = dimnames(kmeans_basic_df)[[2]][-1], clustersum = scale(sapply(X = kmeans_basic_df[kmeans_basic_df$Clusters == i, ], mean)[-1]))
  df.barplot <- rbind(df.barplot, temp)
}


ggplot(data = df.barplot, aes(var, clustersum)) +
  labs(title = "Z-scores of mean number of tags per cluster category") +
  facet_wrap(~cluster) +
  geom_col() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 1, size = 4))

ggsave(filename = "./Images/cluster_vals.png", dpi = "print")
```

2/15/2026: Interesting to note that all POS's tagged as Singular are
higher in Cluster 3, which Att. belongs to. Verbs also look farthest
above the mean in the same cluster, so that is worth investigating as
well. Present tense is by far the most common in Cluster 3, and it only
comes close in cluster 5.

-   Cluster 3 contains only comedy, Sulpicia, and the Letters to Atticus

We also want to know in what ways the letters to Atticus are similar and
different. We need to know what p-value to select, though, because of
multiple testing. We'll be doing 10 tests for 10 variables. The
Bonferroni correction suggests a p-value of \<0.005, because we're doing
10 tests (alpha p-value = 0.05 / number of tests = 10). If this doesn't
work, lets try the Holm-Sidak correction instead.

```{r}
# Start with 2nd person verbs as an example.


cluster4 <- kmeans_basic_df |>
  filter(Clusters == 4)

cluster4 <- rbind(kmeans_basic_df[1, ], cluster4)

# Let's not used the scaled values, because we're comparing means, and the mean would be 0.
att <- cluster4$X2[1] # Atticus is always first
colloquial.non.att <- cluster4$X2[2:length(cluster4$X2)]

print("2nd person:")


# DIFFERENCE IS SIGNIFICANT

# Let's make this a function.
##### The structure will be:
# list$lowertail
# list$uppertail

norm_tails <- function(att, colloquial.non.att, plotname) {
  # Boxplot
  boxplot(colloquial.non.att, att)

  # Perform a t-test
  # Hypothesis: The mean number of second-person verbs is significantly different in the letters to Atticus
  # Null: The mean number of second-person verbs isn't significantly different

  fit <- fitdistr(x = colloquial.non.att, densfun = "normal")

  mean <- fit$estimate["mean"]
  sd <- fit$estimate["sd"]

  X <- seq(from = min(colloquial.non.att), to = max(colloquial.non.att), by = 0.5)
  Y <- dnorm(X, mean = mean, sd = sd)
  {
    hist(colloquial.non.att, freq = FALSE, main = plotname)
    lines(X, Y)
    abline(v = att, col = "red")
  }

  # Percentage of density function above
  # Chances a value is greater
  low <- pnorm(att, mean = mean, sd = sd, lower.tail = FALSE)
  # Chances a value is smaller
  up <- pnorm(att, mean = mean, sd = sd, lower.tail = TRUE)

  return(c(lowertail = low, uppertail = up))
}

norm_tails(att, colloquial.non.att, "2nd person verbs")


# Let's compare the letters to Atticus with Comedy
map(
  cluster4,
  \(x) norm_tails(x[1], x[-1], "")
)

# The result is significant with p < 0.0025 in Present tense, Imperatives, 2nd person, Past and Perfect tense, Finite verbs, Nominative nouns, ablative nouns, adjectives, genitive nouns, prepositions (which are higher in Comedy), passive verbs, and relative pronouns.
# The result is not significantly different in determiners, participles, accusative nouns, personal pronouns, pronouns, 3rd person verbs, and nouns

# Lets compare the letters to Atticus with its own cluster
cluster1 <- kmeans_basic_df |>
  filter(Clusters == 1)

map(
  cluster1,
  \(x) norm_tails(x[1], x[-1], "")
)
# The result is significant with p < 0.0025 in participles, which are lower than the others, pronouns, which are higher.

print("2nd person:")

# remove(cluster4)

# Letters to Atticus can't
```

## CLARA

Clara is another clustering algorithm like K-means, but which uses
medoids instead of means. The medoid is an actual data point at the
center instead of a mean center.

```{r echo=TRUE}
set.seed(126126)

clara.res <- clara(df_clust, k = k, metric = hc_metric, samples = 50, pamLike = TRUE)

fviz_cluster(clara.res,
  data = df_clust, stand = FALSE, palette = c("red", "green", "blue", "purple", "yellow", "magenta", "gray", "cyan"),
  geom = geom,
  ellipse.type = ellipse.type,
  ggtheme = ggtheme,
) + geom_text_repel(aes(label = rownames(df_clust)), vjust = "inward", hjust = "inward", max.overlaps = 12, size = 1)

ggsave(filename = "Images/clara-results.png", dpi = "print")
```

## AGNES

But it might be a good idea to look at other methods of clustering as
well. The graphs above seem to show that K-means gets some well-defined
groupings, but let's test out hcut. I do know that there are many
methods of hclust, so the next step after some more experimentation is
to try that.

We also want to see what creates the tightest clusters. We have a few
parameters we can change:

1.  The number of variables under consideration, like above

2.  The function (hclust, agnes, or diana)

3.  The agglomeration method (ward.D2 is oft-recommended, but it's worth
    seeing what works best)

4.  The metric (I've only seen Euclidean and Manhattan mentioned, I
    don't know which is best)

**Note:** AGNES apparently comes with a value telling us how tight the
clusters are, according to [this
article](https://uc-r.github.io/hc_clustering).

```{r Hierarchical clustering}
# df_clust2 <- df_clust[sample(nrow(df_clust), 80),]
df_clust2 <- df_clust # I've commented out the one above because sometimes I want a sample

hcut_results <- hcut(df_clust2, k = k, hc_func = hc_func, hc_method = hc_method) # k is defined in the first section in Analysis
```

Let's compare different results visually.

```{r}
hcut_res2 <- hcut(df_clust2, k = k, hc_func = "hclust", hc_method = hc_method)

dend1 <- as.dendrogram(hcut_results)
dend2 <- as.dendrogram(hcut_res2)

dendlist <- dendlist(dend1, dend2)

# Align and plot two dendrograms side by side
dendlist(dend1, dend2) %>%
  untangle(method = "step1side") %>% # Find the best alignment layout
  tanglegram(lab.cex = 0.2) %>%
  entanglement() # Draw the two dendrograms

dev.copy(png, "tanglegram.png")
dev.off()
```

```{r}
fviz_nbclust(df_clust2, hcut, method = "wss")
fviz_nbclust(df_clust2, hcut, method = "silhouette")
fviz_nbclust(df_clust2, hcut, method = "gap_stat")

agnes_results <- hcut(df_scaled[, colnames(df_scaled) %in% c("ADP", "2", "Pres", "Fin", "Pass", "Gen")], stand = FALSE, k = 5, hc_func = hc_func, hc_method = hc_method, hc_metric = hc_metric)

rownames(df_scaled) <- rownames(final.frame)
agnes_results <- hcut(df_scaled, stand = FALSE, k = 4, hc_func = hc_func, hc_method = hc_method, hc_metric = hc_metric)

fviz_dend(agnes_results,
  palette = palette,
  geom = geom,
  ellipse.type = ellipse.type,
  type = "rectangle",
  rect = TRUE,
  # horiz=TRUE,
  ggtheme = theme_bw(),
  cex = cex
)


ggsave(filename = "Images/dendrogram_10vars.png", dpi = "print")
```

***Not sure where the rest of this section got transposed from, but I'll
need to fix this later:***

Now, let's do tests to see what the best variables to study are. It
seems that principal component analysis is the way to go for this.

Here are some of the results of looking at PCA. It looks like Person,
Pronouns, Adjectives, Prepositions, Particles, Personal Pronouns,
Tenses, and AUX are very important. The Ablative too might be a good
idea to include

-   Pass one: c("1", "2", "AUX", "Pres", "Part", "Prs") = 0.88916

-   Pass two: c("1", "2", "PROPN", "Fut", "Fin", "Pass") = 0.87284

-   Pass three: c("1", "2", "3", "AUX", "Pres", "Abl") = 0.88998

-   Pass four: c("1", "ADJ", "SCONJ", "Pres", "Pass", "Prs") = 0.87152

-   Pass five: c("1", "ADP", "Fut", "Pres", "Part", "Pass") = 0.88226

-   

    ```         
    c("2", "PART", "Imp", "Pres", "Part", "Abl") = 0.90210
    ```

This last one has the best results, but what if I add a few more? It
doesn't include Pass, Fut., first person, Prs. I'll try it out in the
PCA testing section.

c("2", "PART", "Imp", "Pres", "Part", "Abl", "Pass", "Fut", "1", "Prs")

## Visualization and Significance Testing

```{r}
# Lets look at boxplots for each cluster:
# Cluster 4 is Att

kmeans_basic_df$Clusters <- as.factor(kmeans_basic_df$Clusters)

ggplot(kmeans_basic_df, aes(Clusters, X2)) +
  geom_boxplot()

ggplot(kmeans_basic_df, aes(Clusters, Prs)) +
  geom_boxplot()

ggplot(kmeans_basic_df, aes(Clusters, Pres)) +
  geom_boxplot()

ggplot(kmeans_basic_df, aes(Clusters, Imp)) +
  geom_boxplot()

ggplot(kmeans_basic_df, aes(Clusters, Part)) +
  geom_boxplot()

ggplot(kmeans_basic_df, aes(Clusters, Abl)) +
  geom_boxplot()

ggplot(kmeans_basic_df, aes(Clusters, Pass)) +
  geom_boxplot()
```

## Testing

This section is for a variety of tests. I look at which parameters
generate the tightest clusters in this first section, and then I select
random variables and see which combinations give the highest Proportion
of Variance in the first 2 principal components. I use this to try and
identify the best subset of variables to look at. Then, I use PCA again,
this time manually looking at the rotations and removing variables which
have the lowest value. This method actually seems to have less success
than my try-random-variables method, as the best PoV I could explain was
in the 70s%, as opposed to the random selection was in the 80s% or even
around 90%.

```{r}
### TESTING WITH AGNES############################
# methods to assess
m <- c("average", "single", "complete", "ward") # note that 'ward' = hclust's 'ward.D2
names(m) <- m

funcs <- c("agnes")
names(funcs) <- funcs

metrics <- c("euclidean", "manhattan", "maximum")
names(metrics) <- metrics


# function to compute coefficient
ac_method <- function(metric, func) {
  as.data.frame(map_df(m, ac_final, metric = metric, func = func))
}

ac_final <- function(method, metric, func) {
  as.data.frame(hcut(df_clust2, hc_func = func, hc_method = method, hc_metric = metric)$ac)
}

past_vals <- function(func, metric, method) {
  strings <- vector(mode = "character")
  for (f in 1:length(func)) {
    for (metr in 1:length(metric)) {
      for (meth in 1:length(method)) {
        strings <- append(strings, paste(func[f], metric[metr], method[meth]))
      }
    }
  }
  return(strings)
}


ac_metric <- function(func) {
  as.data.frame(map_df(metrics, ac_method, func = func))
}

ac <- map_df(funcs, ac_metric)
ac$names <- past_vals(funcs, metrics, m)
View(ac)

# This seems to show that Ward is always the best, no matter the distance measure. If we use agnes, that is.

#   average    single  complete      ward  weighted
# 0.7851783 0.7154408 0.8671490 0.9637023 0.8008371
# As expected, ward got the best results


#############################################
# Test groups of two variables

# Test groups of three variables

# Test groups of four variables

# Test groups of five variables

# (We'll stop there, a permutation sampling 5 from 15 already has over 300,000 variations)

# INDEX is a character vector including columns to select
get_ac <- function(INDEX) {
  agnes(df_scaled[, colnames(df_scaled) %in% INDEX], metric = "euclidean", stand = FALSE, method = "ward")$ac
}

# Start testing combinations.

# This function accepts as a parameter the number of variables to sample from the total. It looks at every combination possible with that sample, and picks the top 25 which have the highest ac (see agnes.object in the help pages for more details). To summarize, ac = i - m(i), with i being an observation, and m(i) = d(C1) / d(Cn), with d(C1) being the dissimilarity with the first singleton cluster, and d(Cn) being the dissimilarity with the final, agglomerated cluster. The interpretation of ac is the higher the value, the better the model fit the data into clusters
lowest_spread <- function(sample_var) {
  combination <- combn(colnames(df_scaled), sample_var, simplify = FALSE)
  combination <- sample(combination, 190)
  ac_list <- lapply(X = combination, FUN = get_ac)
  names(ac_list) <- combination
  ac_vec <- unlist(ac_list)
  sort(ac_vec, decreasing = TRUE)[1:25]
}

#### !NOTE that the following items will get slightly different results every time!###

# Pick groups of 2 variables
lowest_spread(2)

# Pick groups of 3 variables
three.sample <- lowest_spread(sample_var = 3)

# Pick groups of 4 variables
four.sample <- lowest_spread(4)

# Pick groups of 5 variables
five.sample <- lowest_spread(5)

# Pick groups of 6 variables
six.sample <- lowest_spread(6)

#############################################

# Now, let's check the pca and see whether we can get a higher value

get_pca <- function(INDEX) {
  pca.data <- prcomp(df_scaled[, colnames(df_scaled) %in% INDEX])
  return(summary(pca.data)$importance[3, 2])
}

highest_pca <- function(sample_var) {
  combination <- combn(colnames(df_scaled), sample_var, simplify = FALSE)
  combination <- sample(combination, 300)
  ac_list <- lapply(X = combination, FUN = get_pca)
  names(ac_list) <- combination
  ac_vec <- unlist(ac_list)
  sort(ac_vec, decreasing = TRUE)[1:25]
}

six.sample <- highest_pca(6)
six.sample

seven.sample <- highest_pca(7)
seven.sample
```

Now that I've read up a bit more in PCA in R, I know that I can actually
see the strength of correlation for each individual variable

```{r}
#
# pca <- prcomp(df_scaled) #NOTE: Should try making the rank. argument smaller, maybe 5?
#
# View(pca$rotation)
#
# pca <- df_scaled %>% select(-Rcp, -Tot, -Cmp, -Con, -Art, -Voc, -Prosp, -PROPN, -NUM) %>% prcomp
# summary(pca)
#
# View(pca$rotation)
#
# pca <- df_scaled %>% select(-Rcp, -Tot, -Cmp, -Con, -Art, -Voc, -Prosp, -PROPN, -NUM, -Masc, -Fem, -Neut, -CCONJ) %>% prcomp
# summary(pca)
# View(pca$rotation)
#
# #Combine the correlation strength from the first two principal components, let's see if we can remove more
# cor <- abs(as.data.frame(pca$rotation)$PC1) + abs(as.data.frame(pca$rotation)$PC2)
# names(cor) <- rownames(as.data.frame(pca$rotation))
# sort(cor)
#
# remove(cor)
#
# #Try with just the top 10 variables
# pca <- df_scaled %>% select(NOUN, PRON, Rel, Sub, Dem, Past) %>% prcomp
# summary(pca)
# View(pca$rotation)
#
# pca <- df_scaled %>% select(-Rcp, -Tot, -Cmp, -Con, -Art, -Voc, -Prosp, -PROPN, -NUM, -Masc, -Fem, -Neut, -CCONJ, -ADV, -Acc, -Loc, -Dat, -Nom) %>% prcomp
# summary(pca)
# View(pca$rotation)
#
# pca <- df_scaled %>% select(c(`2`, "PART", "Imp", "Pres", "Part", "Abl", "Pass", "Fut", "1", "Prs")) %>% prcomp(rank. = 2)
# summary(pca)
# View(pca$rotation)
#
# #Try with the same variables as above
# #df_test <- df_scaled %>% select(-Rcp, -Tot, -Cmp, -Con, -Art, -Voc, -Prosp, -PROPN, -NUM, -Masc, -Fem, -Neut, -CCONJ, -ADV, -Acc, -Loc, -Dat, -Nom)
#
# #Try this with a new subset of variables
# df_test <- df_scaled %>% select(c(`2`, "PART", "Imp", "Pres", "Part", "Abl", "Pass", "Fut", "1", "Prs"))
#
# fviz_cluster(kmeans(df_test, centers = k, nstart = 50), data=df_test, stand = FALSE, palette = c("red", "green", "blue", "purple", "yellow", "magenta", "gray", "cyan"),
#              geom = geom,
#              ellipse.type = ellipse.type,
#              ggtheme = ggtheme,
#              ) + geom_text_repel(aes(label=rownames(df_clust)), vjust="inward", hjust="inward", max.overlaps = 12, size=2)
#
# km.test <- kmeans(df_test, centers = 8, nstart = 50)
# df.test <- data.frame(clusters = km.test$cluster, df_test)
# df.test$title <- final.frame$title
#
#
# remove(km.test)
# remove(df.test)
#
#
# ggsave(filename = "Images/pca_test_cluster.png", dpi="print")
```

Given the previous section, let's only remove the following items:

-   VERB

-   CCONJ

-   X

-   NOUN

-   ADJ

-   Sing

-   Plur

-   Act

-   All cases but Abl

-   Degree

-   Neg

```{r}
fviz_nbclust(df_clust2, hcut, method = "wss")
fviz_nbclust(df_clust2, hcut, method = "silhouette")
fviz_nbclust(df_clust2, hcut, method = "gap_stat")

agnes_results <- hcut(df_scaled[, colnames(df_scaled) %in% c("ADP", "2", "Pres", "Fin", "Pass", "Gen")], stand = FALSE, k = 5, hc_func = hc_func, hc_method = hc_method, hc_metric = hc_metric)

rownames(df_scaled) <- rownames(final.frame)
agnes_results <- hcut(df_scaled, stand = FALSE, k = 4, hc_func = hc_func, hc_method = hc_method, hc_metric = hc_metric)

fviz_dend(agnes_results,
  palette = palette,
  geom = geom,
  ellipse.type = ellipse.type,
  type = "rectangle",
  rect = TRUE,
  # horiz=TRUE,
  ggtheme = theme_bw(),
  cex = cex
)


ggsave(filename = "Images/dendrogram_10vars.png", dpi = "print")
```

### Significance testing

```{r}
cluster.aov <- kmeans_basic_df

# F-test
f_tests <- cluster.aov |>
  dplyr::select(-Clusters) |>
  map_dfr(
    \(x) var.test(
      x = x[cluster.aov$Clusters == 1],
      y = x[cluster.aov$Clusters == 4],
      alternative = "two.sided"
    ),
    .id = TRUE
  )
# Only Participles, Passive verbs, and Future tense verbs are significantly different, with a lot more variance in participles in cluster 4


# Did this without apply() for some reason, this looks at anovas
# cluster.aov <- kmeans_basic_df[kmeans_basic_df$Clusters %in% c(2,4),] #only look at clusters 1 and 4
# cluster.aov$Clusters <- as.character(cluster.aov$Clusters)
#
# aov.res <- aov(X2 ~ Clusters, data = cluster.aov)
#
# summary(aov.res)
#
#
# aov.res <- aov(PART ~ Clusters, data = cluster.aov)
#
# aov.res <- aov(Imp ~ Clusters, data = cluster.aov)
# summary(aov.res)
#
# aov.res <- aov(Pres ~ Clusters, data = cluster.aov)
#
# aov.res <- aov(Part ~ Clusters, data = cluster.aov)
#
# aov.res <- aov(Abl ~ Clusters, data = cluster.aov)
#
# aov.res <- aov(Pass ~ Clusters, data = cluster.aov)
#
# aov.res <- aov(Fut ~ Clusters, data = cluster.aov)
#
# aov.res <- aov(X1 ~ Clusters, data = cluster.aov)
#
# aov.res <- aov(Prs ~ Clusters, data = cluster.aov)

# T-test
# Use map, because I was an idiot not to do that up above
t_tests <- cluster.aov |>
  dplyr::select(-Clusters) |>
  map_dfr(
    \(x) t.test(
      x = x[cluster.aov$Clusters == 1],
      y = x[cluster.aov$Clusters == 4],
      alternative = "two.sided"
    ),
    .id = TRUE
  )

# Means are significantly different in all cases except in Determiners, Accusative nouns, and Nominative nouns. What about effect size? Can't do that with differently sized samples.
```

### Better variable selection

At the beginning of this section, I used PCA to get my best guess at a
selection of variables. However, I want a better method, and I've been
reading Moisl 2015 in order to get a better method. On p. 69, he says
*frequency, variability* and *nonrandomness* are the best criteria for
variables.

First, lets look at frequency.

```{r Frequency}
final.frame |>
  dplyr::select(-title) |>
  map(sum) |>
  unlist() |>
  sort(decreasing = TRUE)

#############
# top 30
# NOUN         Masc          Nom          Imp          Fin          Acc          Act          Fem            3
# 4.435668e+05 4.147746e+05 3.100554e+05 2.736535e+05 2.730731e+05 2.730541e+05 2.627717e+05 2.541429e+05 2.259616e+05
#         Neut          Abl         Pres         Perf          ADJ         PRON          DET         Pass         Part
# 2.008808e+05 1.945355e+05 1.621975e+05 1.401730e+05 1.358719e+05 1.119248e+05 1.106535e+05 1.093912e+05 1.014122e+05
#          ADP         Past          Gen          ADV          Prs        CCONJ          Rel        SCONJ            2
# 9.528447e+04 9.236044e+04 9.118092e+04 8.923980e+04 8.155264e+04 7.987062e+04 7.248893e+04 7.220167e+04 6.811335e+04
```

Then the variance, since every variable is just measured by frequency,
i.e. on the same scale:

```{r}
final.frame |>
  dplyr::select(-title) |>
  map(var) |>
  unlist() |>
  sort(decreasing = TRUE)

########################
# Top 30
# Pres         NOUN         Masc          Imp            2          Act          Fin          Nom          Abl
# 2.831353e+05 2.716960e+05 2.637017e+05 2.084504e+05 1.756891e+05 1.259599e+05 1.175200e+05 1.033145e+05 1.003215e+05
#         Past          Fem         Perf            3         PRON         Neut          Prs          Acc            1
# 9.743705e+04 9.480298e+04 9.071634e+04 8.927443e+04 6.354106e+04 5.912976e+04 5.849000e+04 5.831359e+04 5.334554e+04
#          ADJ          Gen          ADP         Part         Pass        CCONJ          DET          Rel          Sub
# 4.641446e+04 3.933973e+04 3.639815e+04 2.903627e+04 2.749880e+04 2.301436e+04 2.004029e+04 1.736851e+04 1.405187e+04
```

Now, for the non-randomness parameter:

```{r}
final.frame |>
  dplyr::select(-title) |>
  map(\(x) var(x) / mean(x)) |>
  unlist() |>
  sort(decreasing = TRUE)
```

## Notes on Specific Tags

I want to see whether some of my observations from the thesis bear out.
For example, what if we only look at participles? Here are the lowest 25
texts by participle count:

```{r}
final.frame %>%
  slice_min(Part, n = 25) %>%
  tidytable::select(title) %>%
  print(n = 25)
```

Notice how it's just comedy and the letters to Atticus!!!

But what about other letters? Let's look at Pliny's letters

```{r}
final.frame %>%
  slice_min(Part, n = 229) %>%
  tidytable::select(title) %>%
  print(n = 229)
```

## QA of Postags

I took a selection of about 250 random words from the corpus. For each
field, I put a '1' if the tag was correct for that word and a '0' if the
tag was incorrect. If the TAG was incorrect, messing with the rest of
the results, I put a 0 for the rest. This means the results will
actually be harsher than they ought to be, as there will probably be
more incorrect "NumType" arguments, for example, than there are even
instances of the "NumType" field being used.

Anecdotally, the biggest challenges were confusing dative and ablative
(usually counting as ablative when it should be dative), or the
algorithm struggled to get the correct lemma, often making one up when
it did make a mistake. Usually, getting the wrong lemma didn't actually
cause the parsing to be incorrect. It only did so when the algorithm
thought a word was a part of speech that it simply wasn't, saying a noun
was a verb for example. So, it might say "terra" is from some lemma
"terrum", but it still says it's Nominative, Singular, feminine. Words
with two forms were sometimes a problem; *cum* got mistagged once.
*Modo* was mistagged as *modus* instead of the adverb.

Another major challenge is the fact Stanza doesn't recognize enclitics
like -que and -ve as the old CLTK did.

Unfortunately, I had to do some reprocessing. Even though the index of
the row in the original data is saved(in the data frame in the code
snippet below, it is the \$index column), it might not correspond
anymore. For many of them it still should, but possibly not all,
especially in Livy.

Note: If I were to do the QA again, I would make sure to better consider
these situations:

-   Select even numbers of each part of speech (n / number of parts of
    speech = sample to select from each)

-   Mark use of *sum* as AUX as correct. I've been saying it's
    incorrect, based on the way LDT works, not UD

-   If the TAG is completely wrong, then there needs to be a third NA
    option for the rest of the tags. The mistagging will invalidate
    whether the other fields are useful.

    -   What about circumstances like *liberi,* where it means
        "children." It might get the lemma and the tag wrong, but I
        still would consider it a success if it gets the result *masc.,
        nom., plur.*

-   Tag for when a tag is correctly left off, like having an empty TENSE
    tag on a noun

-   Save the value of the TAG. That way, I can see how many NOUNS had
    correct number. Currently, looking at the correct tags for number
    looks at nouns, verbs, adjectives, pronouns.

-   Make sure I'm consistent about tagging Tense correctly when it's
    empty for infinitives. A present infinitive always gets Imperfective
    aspect, and a perfect infinitive gets Perfective.

    Another potential issue is where it tags correctly, but the
    limitation is really in the tagging system itself. For example, *ius
    iurandum* is really a single noun. To treat *iurandum* as an example
    of a gerund really ignores the fact that the phrase works together.
    However, it's not incorrect to tag it that way morphologically.

```{r}
setwd("~/GitHub/cltk-2025-atticus")
qa <- read.csv("postag-tests.csv", header = TRUE)

qa |>
  dplyr::select(-path, -index, -context) |>
  map(\(x) prop.table(table(x)) * 100)
```

# Misc Tests

I want to understand K-means clustering better, so I'm gonna play around
with it here

```{r Kmeans tests}
frame <- data.frame(a = rnorm(10, mean = 10), b = rnorm(10, mean = 20))

scaled <- scale(frame)

scaled

km_result <- kmeans(scaled, centers = 2, nstart = 5)
km_table <- data.frame(km_result$cluster, km_result$centers)

fviz_cluster(km_result,
  data = scaled, palette = c("gray", "red"),
  geom = "point",
  ellipse.type = "convex",
  ggtheme = theme_bw(),
)
```

And some ggplot2 experiments based on the book. Here's some of my
playing around with ch. 8.

```{r}
df <- data.frame(x = 1, y = 3:1, family = c("sans", "serif", "mono"))

df

ggplot(df, aes(x, y)) +
  geom_text(aes(label = family, family = family))

fviz_cluster(kmeans_basic_cluster,
  data = df_clust, palette = c("#2E9FDF", "#00AFBB", "#E7B800", "red", "gray"),
  geom = "point",
  ellipse.type = "convex",
  ggtheme = theme_bw(),
) + geom_text_repel(aes(label = rownames(df_clust)), vjust = "inward", hjust = "inward", check_overlap = TRUE, size = 3)

fviz_dend(hcut_results,
  palette = c("#2E9FDF", "#00AFBB", "#E7B800", "red"),
  geom = "point",
  ellipse.type = "convex",
  type = "rectangle",
  # type = "phylogenic",
  # repel = TRUE,
  rect = TRUE,
  cex = 0.4,
  ggtheme = theme_bw()
)

fviz_dend(hcut_results,
  k = 4, palette = c("#2E9FDF", "#00AFBB", "#E7B800", "red"),
  ellipse.type = "convex",
  type = "phylogenic",
  repel = TRUE,
  cex = 0.7,
  ggtheme = theme_bw()
)
```

Some crucial notes on the previous section:

-   Adding the check_overlap argument to geom_text() in fviz_cluster()
    is very useful. It removes overlapping labels, privileging those
    that are higher in the data frame (meaning the letters to Atticus
    are usually visible)

-   Reduce cex to 0.7 for readability

## How K-Means Clustering Works

K-Means clustering itself is relatively simple. The computer has a set
of data, and it wants to determine which observations are closer to each
other than another.

Let's first imagine we're talking about actual distance

| Point | X Coordinate | Y Coordinate |
|-------|--------------|--------------|
| A     | 1            | 0            |
| B     | 2            | 0            |
| C     | 1            | 10           |
| D     | 2            | 10           |
| E     | 3            | 10           |

Let's graph these points:

```{r}
df <- data.frame(X.Coordinate = c(1, 2, 1, 2, 3), Y.Coordinate = c(0, 0, 10, 10, 10), row.names = LETTERS[1:5])

ggplot(data = df, aes(x = X.Coordinate, y = Y.Coordinate)) +
  geom_point()
```

Okay, so we want the computer to recognize that there are two groups of
points close together, the ones at the top and the bottom.

The K-means algorithm lets the computer recognize there are two
clusters.

```{r}
km <- kmeans(df, centers = 2, nstart = 5)

fviz_cluster(km, data = df)
```

But we're not looking at distances, we're looking at data. However, we
can apply the same method to data that doesn't represent an actual
distance.

Let's say we have a bunch of students, and each take two classes. We
want to sort the students into groups, but how do we do that when they
have two grades in different classes? We could do it by eye, but what if
they were taking 4 classes? 5 classes? How can we objectively sort them
into groups?

Starting with the simplest case, here are some grades:

| Student | English | Math |
|---------|---------|------|
| Sally   | 95      | 89   |
| Susan   | 96      | 72   |
| Sam     | 55      | 78   |
| Shelly  | 76      | 75   |
| Stu     | 40      | 96   |

We can plot these values in space, even though they are

```{r}
df <- data.frame(English = c(95, 96, 55, 76, 40), Math = c(89, 72, 78, 75, 96), row.names = c("Sally", "Susan", "Sam", "Shelly", "Stu"))

ggplot(df, aes(x = English, y = Math)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_text(aes(label = rownames(df)), nudge_y = -2)
```

Well, let's do the same clustering algorithm again.

NOTE: I tried it again without scaling, the results are similar, but
different. Now, Stu is matched up with Sam. (The eagle-eyed among you
might notice there's an extra step in here. If you're curious, we needed
to scale the values (subtract the mean and divide by the standard
deviation) so that they're comparable with each other.)

```{r}
# scaled_df = scale(df)

km <- kmeans(df, centers = 2, nstart = 2)

fviz_cluster(km, data = df)
```

Notice how the other students are similar to each other because they're
scores in their two classes are pretty close together. Stu, however, has
one high and one low value (he really doesn't like English), so he's
very different from the others.

R is great because it also handles data with more than two dimensions.
If each student had many classes (Math, English, History, Science,
Music, Art), then we could use principal component analysis to plot
differences between these datasets in 2D space. That way, K-means works
even for large datasets.

| Student | English | Math | History | Science |
|---------|---------|------|---------|---------|
| Sally   | 95      | 89   | ..      | ..      |
| Susan   | 96      | 72   | ..      | ..      |
| Sam     | 55      | 78   | ..      | ..      |
| Shelly  | 76      | 75   | ..      | ..      |
| Stu     | 40      | 96   | ..      | ..      |
